title: SEGMENT 13 — Trodelvy and the TROP2 story

lines.text: SEGMENT 13 — Trodelvy and the TROP2 story
Sacituzumab govitecan, marketed as Trodelvy, was approved by the FDA in 2020 for metastatic triple-negative breast cancer.
It is the leading example of an ADC that targets TROP2 — and TROP2 is one of the most important new targets in oncology.
Let me set up the clinical problem.
Triple-negative breast cancer is the subtype of breast cancer that lacks expression of all three of the major druggable receptors — estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, and HER2.
About fifteen percent of breast cancers are triple-negative, and they tend to behave aggressively.
Because they lack the targets that the other breast cancer subtypes are treated against, these patients have historically had limited options beyond cytotoxic chemotherapy.
Triple-negative disease was, until recently, the breast cancer subtype that had benefited least from precision medicine.
Trodelvy targets TROP2, which stands for trophoblast cell surface antigen 2.

TROP2 is a transmembrane glycoprotein expressed at low levels on many normal epithelial tissues and at much higher levels on a wide variety of carcinomas — breast, lung, urothelial, gastric, ovarian, you name it.
It's overexpressed on essentially all triple-negative breast cancers.

As an ADC target, TROP2 is attractive because it's broad — many tumor types express it — but it has the wrinkle that it's also expressed at low levels on many normal tissues, so the therapeutic window can be tight.
The antibody in Trodelvy is sacituzumab, a humanized anti-TROP2 IgG.
The payload is SN-38, which is the active metabolite of irinotecan — a topoisomerase I inhibitor in the camptothecin family.
The linker is an interesting choice — it's a hydrolyzable carbonate linker designed to be moderately stable in plasma but to slowly release SN-38 even in circulation.
This is unusual.
Most modern ADCs aim for very tight plasma stability.
Trodelvy's linker is deliberately leakier than, say, T-DXd's.
Why?
Because it gives Trodelvy a different kind of bystander effect.
The free SN-38 generated by slow linker hydrolysis is small, membrane-permeable, and cytotoxic — so even before the ADC reaches the tumor, some payload is being released and circulating.
This sounds like a bug, but it's part of the design.

Trodelvy has high DAR — about 7.6 — and the combination of high DAR plus a moderately leaky linker delivers a lot of free SN-38 to the tumor environment, where it can kill TROP2-low cells that the antibody itself wouldn't efficiently target.
Of course, this design choice also drives toxicity.
The dose-limiting side effects of Trodelvy include severe diarrhea — characteristic of free SN-38 — and neutropenia.
Both of these are reversible with supportive care, but they require careful clinical management.
Clinically, Trodelvy has been a real win.
In metastatic triple-negative breast cancer that had failed prior therapy, Trodelvy extended overall survival by about five months compared to chemotherapy of physician's choice.
That's a meaningful benefit in a population that previously had nothing.
It's now approved for triple-negative breast cancer, HR-positive HER2-negative breast cancer, and metastatic urothelial cancer.
The bigger story is what Trodelvy proved about TROP2 as a target.
After Trodelvy validated TROP2, a whole wave of follow-on TROP2-targeted ADCs entered development.
Daiichi's datopotamab deruxtecan, which we mentioned in the last segment, is anti-TROP2 with a DXd payload — a different chemistry from Trodelvy.
Several other companies have TROP2 ADCs in trials with various payloads and linkers.
The space is crowded and competitive, and the eventual winners will be determined by which combination of antibody, payload, linker, and DAR produces the best therapeutic window in each indication.
Trodelvy is also a study in how a single drug can validate an entire target class.
Before Trodelvy, TROP2 was a relatively obscure target that some pharmacologists thought might be tractable.
After Trodelvy, every major oncology company has a TROP2 program.
That's the leverage that a successful ADC can have on the broader research agenda.
